nuggets.headline: Daft Punk's world-conquering debut album was named "Homework" for a brutally literal reason.
nuggets.text: While their later albums were multi-million dollar studio epics, the duo's 1997 debut *Homework* was famously recorded in Thomas Bangalter's bedroom. The title was a direct nod to this DIY process, where the two 22-year-olds crafted revolutionary tracks like "Around the World" using a modest setup of synths and samplers, completely outside the traditional studio system. This lo-fi, self-contained approach is what gives the album its distinctively raw and unpolished texture.

source.title: Daft Punk - Homework, The Blueprint That Rewrote Electronic Music's DNA - Decoded Magazine
source.quoteSnippet: Thomas Bangalter and Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo, both all of 22 years old, mind you, had just unleashed Homework upon an unsuspecting world.

nuggets.headline: The iconic robotic vocal on "Around the World" is a clever piece of analog trickery, not the digital effect everyone assumes.
nuggets.text: Many listeners mistake the synthesized vocal hook for an early example of Auto-Tune, but the track was released in early 1997. The widespread, overt use of Auto-Tune as a creative effect wouldn't popularize until Cher's "Believe" in late 1998. Daft Punk actually achieved their futuristic sound using a talk box, a classic analog effect where an instrument's sound is fed through a tube into the performer's mouth, allowing them to "shape" the notes with their voice—a perfect example of their philosophy of blending vintage gear with forward-thinking ideas.

source.title: How Auto-Tune Revolutionized the Sound of Popular Music
source.quoteSnippet: The song, of course, was Cher’s “Believe,” a worldwide smash on its October 1998 release. ... The pitch-correction technology Auto-Tune had been on the market for about a year before “Believe” hit the charts, but its previous appearances had been discreet.

nuggets.headline: If the raw, filtered funk of "Around the World" is your ideal sound, you need to meet the other architect of the French Touch, Etienne de Crécy.
nuggets.text: As Daft Punk were crafting *Homework*, another Parisian genius, Etienne de Crécy, was creating a parallel masterpiece with his project Superdiscount. His 1997 album of the same name is a cornerstone of the "French Touch" sound, built from the exact same ingredients: heavily compressed drums, infectious filtered disco loops, and hypnotic, repetitive arrangements. Tracks like "Prix Choc" feel like a direct conversation with Daft Punk's early work, sharing a raw, joyful, and relentlessly groovy DNA.

source.title: Daft Punk - Homework, The Blueprint That Rewrote Electronic Music's DNA - Decoded Magazine
source.quoteSnippet: across the channel, two French lads were quietly crafting what would become a significant shift in how we understood dance music.

artistSummary: Daft Punk, the duo of Thomas Bangalter and Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo, fundamentally reshaped electronic music by fusing the analog warmth of '70s funk and disco with the raw energy of Chicago house and techno. Their 1997 debut *Homework* was a lo-fi masterpiece that brought the "French Touch" sound to global consciousness, establishing a blueprint for dance music that was both nostalgic and futuristic. They are celebrated for their musical innovation, visual artistry, and an enigmatic robot persona that prioritized the music over celebrity.
